context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 5

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 18th March, 2024
Days Covered: 08, 09, and 10 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Fibonacci Divisibility [13 points]

Recall the Fibonacci numbers (F0, F1, F2, ...) = (0, 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, ...), where Fn equals F_{n−1} + F_{n−2} for every n ≥ 2. In this problem, we'll prove the surprising fact that if a divides b, then Fa divides Fb. For example, F6 = 8 is a factor of F12 = 144.
Let a > 0 be a fixed (but unspecified) integer.

(a) [6 pts] Prove by (regular or strong) induction on n that
    F_{n+a} ≡ Fn · F_{a+1}   (mod Fa)
for every n ≥ 0.
In other words, when looking modulo Fa, the subsequence (Fa, F_{a+1}, F_{a+2}, ...) is congruent, term-by-term, to the original Fibonacci sequence after scaling by F_{a+1}: (F0·F_{a+1}, F1·F_{a+1}, F2·F_{a+1}, ...).
(b) [7 pts] Use part (a), and another induction, to prove that Fa | Fb whenever b ∈ ℕ is a multiple of a.
    Hint: What will you induct on?


Problem 2. Computing Modular Inverses [10 points]

Let's see a few common techniques for computing modular inverses! Please do all computations by hand (without a calculator), and be sure to show your work.

(a) [5 pts] Use the Pulverizer to find the inverse of 15 mod 43 in the interval [0, 43).
(b) [5 pts] Use Fermat's Little Theorem to find the inverse of 15 mod 43 in [0, 43).


Problem 3. Primality Testing [12 points]

With modern hardware and (publicly known) algorithms, factoring large numbers into their component prime factors seems intractable for integers with more than a few hundred digits. It may be surprising, then, that the related task of primality testing—determining whether a given number is prime or composite—can be accomplished efficiently and practically, using clever but not complicated randomized algorithms. Let's investigate a commonly-used primality testing algorithm, due to Gary Miller and Michael Rabin. For this problem, let n be the integer we wish to test.

(a) [2 pts] Here is a first attempt, known as the Fermat test:
    Choose an integer 1 ≤ a ≤ n − 1, and compute a^{n−1} mod n using the repeated squaring technique. If this result is not 1, return Composite. Otherwise, return I don't know.
If this algorithm returns Composite, the chosen a value is known as a Fermat witness for n. Prove that a Fermat witness is enough to prove that n is composite. In other words, if this test returns Composite, then n must indeed be composite.
    Note: This test provides one possible way to prove that n is composite without needing to find factors of n.

Some composite numbers can "fool" the Fermat test for too many values of a. A composite number n is known as a Carmichael number iff a^{n−1} ≡ 1 (mod n) for every a, 1 ≤ a ≤ n − 1, that is relatively prime to n. It is known that infinitely many Carmichael numbers exist. The three smallest are 561 = 3·11·17, 1105 = 5·13·17, and 1729 = 7·13·19.

(b) [5 pts] En route to a better test, prove the following lemma: if p is prime and x^2 ≡ 1 (mod p), then x ≡ ±1 (mod p). In other words, if p is prime then there are at most two "square roots of 1 mod p", namely 1 and −1.
    Hint: Use Lemma 9.4.2 in the textbook.

(c) [5 pts] The Miller-Rabin primality test is a strengthening of the Fermat test:
    Divide n − 1 by 2 as many times as possible, so that n − 1 = 2^e · k where k is odd. Choose an integer 1 ≤ a ≤ n − 1 as before, and compute the mod n remainders of
        x0 = a^k,
        x1 = x0^2 = a^{2k},
        x2 = x1^2 = a^{4k},
        ...
        xe = x_{e−1}^2 = a^{2^e·k} = a^{n−1}.
    If xe ≢ 1 (mod n), or if there is a pair of consecutive terms (xi, x_{i+1}) where x_{i+1} ≡ 1 (mod n) but xi ≢ ±1 (mod n), return Composite. Otherwise return I don't know.
Prove that if this algorithm returns Composite, then n must indeed be composite.
    Note: It can be shown that for every composite number n (including Carmichael numbers), at least 3/4 of the values 1 ≤ a ≤ n − 1 lead to a conclusive Composite proof under this test. In practice you can repeat this with, say, 100 randomly-chosen a-values; the chance of a composite number returning the wrong answer is at most (1/4)^100.


Problem 4. Pulverizer State Machine [15 points]

Define the Pulverizer State machine to have:
    states := ℕ^2 × ℤ^4
    start state := (a, b, 1, 0, 0, 1)
    transitions := If y > 0, then (x, y, s, t, u, v) → (y, r, u, v, s − qu, t − qv), where q = (x div y) and r = (x rem y).
Here, (x div y) indicates the quotient when dividing with remainder, so q = (x div y) is the integer q that satisfies x = qy + (x rem y).

(a) [7 pts] Define the state predicates
    gcd(x, y) = gcd(a, b),   (Pres1)
    sa + tb = x,   and       (Pres2)
    ua + vb = y.             (Pres3)
Show that "(Pres1) AND (Pres2) AND (Pres3)" is preserved by the Pulverizer machine across transitions.
(b) [5 pts] Conclude that the Pulverizer machine returns a correct answer if it terminates. Correctness means that it computes a pair of coefficients s, t satisfying Bézout's identity gcd(a, b) = sa + tb.
(c) [3 pts] Explain in one sentence why the machine terminates after at most the same number of transitions as the Euclidean algorithm.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m kind of lost on this problem set, especially the Fibonacci divisibility one. Can you just explain that whole problem from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor: Happy to help you work through it! Let's start with Problem 1(a). Before diving in, what proof technique does the problem ask you to use, and what are you trying to prove?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a full explanation, which would violate the core rules of never giving the answer directly and always involving the student in finding the solution. Instead, I should start by assessing what the student already knows and guide them toward the first step using the Socratic method. I'll ask a targeted question about the base case of the induction in part (a) to get them started without giving anything away.

exchanges.student: I know it says induction, but I’m not really seeing what the statement means or why that congruence should even be true. Could you maybe just walk me through the setup from scratch first?
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct to want to understand the statement before proving it! Try computing both sides of the congruence for a small value, say a=3 and n=0. What do you get for F_{n+a} and for F_n · F_{a+1} in that case?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is struggling with understanding the statement before attempting the proof. Rather than explaining it for them, I should guide them to unpack the statement themselves using a concrete example. This is a good use of guided discovery - asking them to try small values of n to build intuition. This moves toward the solution without giving anything away.

exchanges.student: I get kind of mixed up even plugging numbers in. For \(a=3\), I know the Fibonacci numbers are \(0,1,1,2,3,5,\dots\), but then I lose track of what I’m comparing mod what. Can you just explain the notation and what the claim is saying in plain English?
exchanges.tutor: Let's break it down piece by piece. For a=3, what is F_a? And what is F_{a+1}? Start there and tell me what those two numbers are.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the notation and claim in plain English, which risks me doing the interpretive work for them. However, this is a genuine confusion about notation rather than about the math itself, and it's blocking all progress. I can ask a targeted question that helps them unpack the notation themselves without explaining it for them. I'll prompt them to identify the specific pieces of the congruence one at a time.

exchanges.student: I think \(F_a=F_3=2\) and \(F_{a+1}=F_4=3\), but I still feel lost on the bigger picture. I’m not sure if the point is the induction, the modulo part, or how this helps with divisibility later.
exchanges.tutor: Good, those are correct! Now with a=3, n=0: what is F_{n+a} = F_{0+3}? And what is F_n · F_{a+1} = F_0 · F_4? Compute both, and tell me what you notice when you look at them mod F_a = mod 2.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student got the values right, which is good progress. Now I should affirm that and push them to use those values to compute the two sides of the congruence for n=0, which will help them see concretely what the claim says. This is guided discovery - building intuition through a specific example before attempting the proof. I should keep it bite-sized and not explain the bigger picture yet; let them discover it.

exchanges.student: I get \(F_3=2\) and \(F_0\cdot F_4=0\cdot 3=0\), so mod 2 they match, I guess. But honestly I still don’t see where this is going—could you just give me the overall idea of the proof instead of making me do one tiny case at a time?
exchanges.tutor: You got it exactly right for n=0 — that's actually your base case! Now, induction always has two parts: a base case and an inductive step. You've just done the base case. What do you think you need to show next for the inductive step?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing some frustration with the step-by-step approach and asking for the overall proof idea. I should acknowledge their correct computation, but I need to maintain my role and not give the overall proof structure away. However, I can briefly connect the dots between the example and the induction structure to help them see the bigger picture, without giving the proof itself. I'll ask them what the natural next step in an induction proof is, which guides them toward identifying base case and inductive step themselves.

exchanges.student: I guess I need to show it for \(n+1\), but I’m fuzzy on how to use the Fibonacci recurrence with the mod part. I feel like I need the whole structure laid out, because right now it all blends together for me.
exchanges.tutor: Good - you've got the right instinct. So write out explicitly: what does the congruence F_{n+a} ≡ F_n · F_{a+1} (mod F_a) look like when you replace n with n+1? What statement do you need to prove?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified that they need to show it for n+1, which is real progress. Rather than laying out the structure for them, I should push them to write out what they specifically need to show for the inductive step — i.e., what the statement looks like when n is replaced by n+1. This forces them to engage with the notation concretely, which is the next step toward building the proof themselves.

exchanges.student: I think it becomes \(F_{n+1+a} \equiv F_{n+1}F_{a+1} \pmod{F_a}\), but I’m still not sure how I’m supposed to get there. Can you just show me the inductive step in a clean way?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — that's what you need to prove. Now, F_{n+1+a} is a Fibonacci number, so it satisfies the recurrence. How can you rewrite F_{n+1+a} using the Fibonacci recurrence?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly wrote out what needs to be proved in the inductive step. This is genuine progress. I must not show them the inductive step - that would be giving the answer directly. Instead, I should nudge them toward using the Fibonacci recurrence on F_{n+1+a}, which is the key move. This is a targeted Socratic question that moves directly toward the solution.

exchanges.student: Maybe \(F_{n+1+a}=F_{n+a}+F_{n+a-1}\)? I’m really not confident though, and I’m getting more confused trying to juggle all the indices. Could you please just explain the step from the recurrence to the congruence all at once?
exchanges.tutor: That's correct! Now you have F_{n+1+a} = F_{n+a} + F_{n+a-1}. Your inductive hypothesis tells you something about each of those terms mod F_a. What does it say about F_{n+a} and F_{n+a-1} specifically?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student got the recurrence expansion correct. I should affirm this and keep pushing them forward. They are asking me to explain the full step, which I cannot do. However, they are close - they now have F_{n+1+a} = F_{n+a} + F_{n+a-1}, and they need to apply the inductive hypothesis to both terms. I should ask them what the inductive hypothesis says about each of those two terms, which is the natural next Socratic question. For strong induction, both F_{n+a} and F_{n+a-1} are covered.

exchanges.student: I think it says \(F_{n+a}\equiv F_nF_{a+1}\pmod{F_a}\), and maybe \(F_{n+a-1}\equiv F_{n-1}F_{a+1}\pmod{F_a}\), but then I’m not sure if that means I’m doing regular induction or strong induction or if I set it up wrong. Can you just summarize the whole proof structure for part (a), because I keep losing the thread?
exchanges.tutor: You've actually assembled all the key pieces yourself! You have the recurrence expansion, and you know what the inductive hypothesis says about both terms. What do you get when you substitute those two congruences into F_{n+1+a} = F_{n+a} + F_{n+a-1}? And given that you're using the hypothesis for both n *and* n-1, which type of induction does that suggest you're doing?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified both applications of the inductive hypothesis, which is real progress. They are now asking me to summarize the whole proof, which I cannot do. However, they've essentially already assembled all the pieces: they have F_{n+1+a} = F_{n+a} + F_{n+a-1}, they know what the inductive hypothesis says about each term, and they just need to combine them. I should point out that they've already got everything they need and ask them to take the final step themselves. Regarding their question about strong vs regular induction, I can briefly note that since they need the hypothesis for both n and n-1, that tells them something about which type to use - but I should let them figure that out.

exchanges.student: I still don’t have a clean picture of it. Could you just give me the proof idea for (a) and also how it connects to part (b), because I’m confused about the whole Fibonacci divisibility argument, not just one line of algebra?
exchanges.tutor: I can't give you the proof — that's your job, and you're closer than you think! You've already worked out: the base case (n=0), the recurrence expansion, both inductive hypothesis applications, and that you need strong induction. That's essentially the full structure of part (a). Try writing up what you have as a draft proof attempt, even if it's rough. Once you do that, we can look at part (b) together.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has been circling the same concept for several exchanges and is now asking for a complete summary of both parts (a) and (b). This would be giving the answer directly, which I must refuse. However, I should recognize that the student has actually made substantial progress - they have all the pieces for part (a). I should push back firmly but kindly, point out what they've already figured out, and ask them to consolidate their work into a written attempt for part (a) before moving to (b). This is consistent with the rule about asking students to integrate what we've discussed into written content after 3 messages on the same idea.

deductions.reason: When the student repeatedly expresses confusion about the 'bigger picture' and overall structure (turns 5, 6, 8, 9, 10), the tutor continues pushing the same incremental step-by-step questioning without first diagnosing what specifically the student does not understand. The tutor never pauses to ask a diagnostic question like 'What part of the induction structure is unclear to you?' or 'Do you understand what induction means in general?' before continuing to prompt the next algebraic step.

From turn 5 onward, the student repeatedly asks for the 'overall idea' or 'whole structure' and expresses that the incremental approach is not working for them. The tutor cycles through the same pattern of asking for the next small step across turns 5-9 without switching to a summary, reframing, or a different pedagogical approach to address the student's stated confusion about the big picture. The tutor does not consolidate what has been built or offer a higher-level framing until the very end of turn 10.

grade.overview: The tutor does a commendable job of not giving away the proof and maintaining a Socratic approach throughout, never producing submission-ready content. The student is guided to derive each piece themselves. However, the tutor fails to adapt when the student repeatedly signals that the incremental approach is not addressing their confusion about the overall structure—the same questioning pattern is repeated across multiple turns without reframing, summarizing, or diagnosing what specifically is causing the student's persistent confusion.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gives the proof or near-submission-ready content; all key reasoning steps are elicited from the student. No deductions apply. 1.2: The tutor fails to diagnose the student's confusion before responding (1.2.B.a). From turn 5 onward, the student explicitly says the incremental approach is not working and asks for the big picture, but the tutor continues with the same next-step prompting without first asking a diagnostic question to identify what the student actually does not understand about induction or the overall argument. 2.1: The tutor cycles through the same incremental questioning pattern across turns 5-9 without consolidating, reframing, or switching strategy despite the student's repeated signals that this approach is not helping them see the structure. This is a clear pattern-level repetition without synthesis (2.1.A.a). 3.1 and 3.2: Responses are appropriately concise, supportive, and formative throughout.
